questions.stem: What is the CPU's primary job?
options: Displaying graphics on screen
Fetching, decoding, and executing instructions — the 'brain' that processes all logic
Storing your files permanently
Connecting to the internet
questions.explanation: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) executes the fetch-decode-execute cycle: fetch an instruction from memory, decode what it means, execute it (add, compare, branch, etc.), then repeat. Everything your program does — function calls, loops, conditionals — is translated into these CPU instructions.

questions.stem: What are Tensor Cores on an NVIDIA GPU and why do they matter for AI?
options: They are CPU cores embedded in the GPU for Python code
Specialised hardware units that multiply two 4×4 matrices in one clock cycle — providing 8× higher throughput for matrix operations vs regular CUDA cores
Memory units that store tensors (multidimensional arrays)
Cooling units that prevent the GPU from overheating during training
questions.explanation: Regular CUDA cores perform one multiply-add per cycle. Tensor Cores perform a 4×4 matrix multiply (64 multiply-adds) per cycle — 8× higher throughput for matrix operations. Since every neural network layer is a matrix multiplication, Tensor Cores are the primary reason modern training is 10-100× faster than it was in 2015. They require float16 or bfloat16 inputs and dimensions divisible by 8.

options: How much data the CPU can store
How many instructions the CPU executes per second — 3.5 GHz = 3.5 billion clock cycles per second
How fast data moves between CPU and RAM
The CPU's operating temperature limit
questions.explanation: Clock speed measures cycles per second: 3.5 GHz = 3.5 × 10⁹ cycles/second. Modern CPUs execute 1-4 instructions per cycle (IPC), so a 3.5 GHz CPU with IPC=3 performs ~10 billion instructions/second. GPU clock speeds are lower (1-2 GHz) but irrelevant — GPUs make up for it with thousands of cores running in parallel.

questions.stem: What does it mean when LLM inference is described as 'memory bandwidth bound'?
options: The model is too large to fit in memory
The CPU is slower than the GPU for this task
The GPU's arithmetic units finish quickly and then wait for model weights to arrive from VRAM — the limiting factor is data transfer speed, not computation speed
The Python code is not optimised
questions.explanation: For a 7B model at batch=1, inference performs ~14 GFLOP per token but reads ~14 GB of weights. At H100's 3.35 TB/s bandwidth, the data arrives in ~4ms — but the 2000 TFLOPS compute could finish in ~0.007ms. The GPU must wait for data, not the other way around. Quantisation (reducing bytes per weight) directly improves inference speed in this regime.

questions.stem: Why should matrix dimensions be multiples of 16 (or at least 8) when using GPU Tensor Cores?
options: Python only supports arrays with dimensions that are powers of 2
Tensor Cores operate on 4×4 sub-matrices; hardware efficiency is highest when dimensions align to 16 — misaligned sizes cause padding or fall back to slower CUDA Cores
It's a convention that has no performance impact
The GPU VRAM is physically divided into 16-byte blocks
questions.explanation: Tensor Cores process matrices in 4×4 (or 8×8, 16×16) tiles. When your dimensions are multiples of 16, every tile is fully utilised — no wasted computation. Odd sizes (e.g. 1000×1001) force padding, and some operations fall back to regular CUDA cores (8× slower). In practice: always pad embedding dimensions, hidden sizes, and batch sizes to multiples of 8 or 16.

questions.stem: What is the difference between CUDA Cores and Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) on a GPU?
options: CUDA Cores are software; SMs are hardware
SMs are the processing units on a GPU; each SM contains multiple CUDA Cores (arithmetic units) plus shared memory, an L1 cache, and Tensor Cores
CUDA Cores handle floating-point; SMs handle integer operations
They are the same thing — just different names used by NVIDIA
questions.explanation: An NVIDIA GPU is organised hierarchically. Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) are independent processing units; an H100 has 132 SMs. Each SM contains 128 CUDA Cores (for scalar FP32 operations), 4 Tensor Cores (for matrix FP16/BF16), and 100+ KB of fast shared memory. Shared memory within an SM is what Flash Attention uses to avoid slow HBM reads for intermediate attention values.
